title: Xor Palindrome

content.problemStatement:
A (111-indexed) binary string SSS of length NNN is called a xor palindrome if the value of Si⊕S(N+1−i)S_i \oplus S_{(N + 1 - i)}Si​⊕S(N+1−i)​ is the same for all 1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N.

For example, 000, 111111111111 and 010101010101 are xor palindromes, while 111011101110 and 110101110101110101 are not.

You are given a binary string SSS of length NNN. Determine if it is possible to rearrange it to form a xor palindrome or not.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the length of the binary string SSS.
The second line of each test case contains the binary string SSS containing 000s and 111s only.


Output Format
For each test case, output YES\texttt{YES}YES if it is possible to rearrange SSS to convert it into a xor palindrome. Otherwise output NO\texttt{NO}NO.


You may print each character of YES\texttt{YES}YES and NO\texttt{NO}NO in uppercase or lowercase (for example, yes\texttt{yes}yes, yEs\texttt{yEs}yEs, Yes\texttt{Yes}Yes will be considered identical).
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N – the length of the binary string. The second line contains a binary string S of length N consisting only of characters '0' and '1'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing "YES" if the characters of S can be rearranged to form a xor palindrome, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: "00" already satisfies xor = 0 for the only pair. Test case 2: Rearrange to "0110" gives xor = 1 for every opposite pair. Test case 3: Rearrange to "010" (a palindrome) gives xor = 0. Test case 4: No arrangement yields a constant xor for all pairs.
Length is odd, any palindrome works; the given string is already a palindrome, so xor = 0 for all pairs.
Length 1 always works. For length 2, counts are equal so xor = 1 arrangement exists. For length 6, counts are equal (3 zeros, 3 ones) → xor = 1 arrangement exists.

solution.approach: For a xor palindrome the value Si xor S(N+1-i) must be constant for all i. If N is odd the middle character pairs with itself, forcing the constant to be 0, so the string must be rearranged into a normal palindrome. With binary characters this is always possible for odd N because exactly one of the counts of '0' and '1' is odd. For even N the constant can be 0 or 1. Constant 0 again requires a palindrome, which is possible when both counts are even. Constant 1 requires every opposite pair to consist of one '0' and one '1', which is possible only when the number of zeros equals the number of ones. Hence the answer is YES if N is odd, or if N is even and (cnt0 == cnt1) or (cnt0 and cnt1 are both even). The algorithm just counts zeros and ones and checks these conditions.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides input storage.

code.Java: import java.io.*;import java.util.*;public class Main{public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();int T=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());while(T-- > 0){int N=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());String S=br.readLine().trim();int cnt0=0;for(int i=0;i<N;i++)if(S.charAt(i)=='0')cnt0++;int cnt1=N-cnt0;boolean ok;if(N%2==1){ok=true;}else{ok=(cnt0==cnt1)||((cnt0%2==0)&&(cnt1%2==0));}sb.append(ok?"YES":"NO").append('\n');}System.out.print(sb.toString());}}
